title: Rating Agency Fees: Pay to Play in Public Finance?
abstract: We examine the relationship between credit rating levels and rating agency fees in a public finance market in which rating agencies earn lower fees and face higher disclosure requirements relative to corporate bond and structured finance markets. Controlling for variation in the complexity of credit analysis at the issue level, we find evidence that rating agency conflicts of interest distort credit ratings in the municipal bond market. Unexpectedly expensive ratings are more likely downgraded, and inexpensive ratings are more likely upgraded. The relationship between credit ratings and rating agency fees is driven by issuers who lose access to AAA insurance.

author.Jess Cornaggia*: Smeal College of Business, Pennsylvania State University,USA
author.Kimberly J Cornaggia: Smeal College of Business, Pennsylvania State University,USA
author.Ryan Israelsen: Eli Broad College of Business, Michigan State University,USA

article.1. Institutional Background and Literatu:
JEL G24 - Investment Banking; Venture Capital; Brokerage; Ratings and Ratings Agencies G28 - Government Policy and Regulation Issue Section: Article Editor: Holger Mueller Holger Mueller Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

The purpose of this paper is to test whether conflicts of interest distort credit ratings in the $4 trillion U.S. public finance markets. The issuer-pays compensation structure potentially affects credit ratings in three ways. If credit risk is difficult to assess due to issuer opacity or issue complexity, then credit rating agencies (CRAs) may produce noisy though unbiased ratings from which the issuer can purchase only the most favorable. This selection is known as “rating shopping.” Alternatively, CRAs may produce biased ratings that favor issuers who generate the most rating business. Results fromGriffin, Nickerson, and Tang (2013)indicate that the inaccurate ratings resulting in the crisis of 2008 were the result of such “rating catering” rather than rating shopping. Certain bond issuers allege a third conflict where CRAs coerce business by producing biased unfavorable ratings for issuers that do not hire them.1The model fromFulghieri, Strobl, and Xia (2013)predicts that CRAs build reputation capital by awarding harsh ratings to issuers that do not pay, which they spend by catering to lucrative issuers.

Prior research has established conflicts of interest distorting credit ratings in corporate bonds (e.g.,Baghai and Becker 2018;Herpfer and Maturana 2020) and structured finance markets (e.g.,Griffin and Tang 2011;Efing and Hau 2015). However, CRA incentives potentially differ in public finance markets for two reasons. First, the ratings revenue generated in public finance markets is smaller than that generated in the more lucrative asset classes previously examined (seeCornaggia, Cornaggia, and Hund 2017). This distinction is important because rating catering is more likely to manifest when high short-run profit exceeds long-term reputation value (seeBolton, Freixas, and Shapiro 2012;Mathis, McAndrews, and Rochet 2009).

Second, the municipal issuers we study are required to publicly disclose the fees they pay to each CRA for each new bond issue. The disclosure of fees is a unique feature of our setting. To our knowledge, no other asset class (e.g., corporate bonds or structured finance markets) provides information on fees paid to specific CRAs. Therefore, the data provide an opportunity to directly test a relationship between ratings and fees. Because any abnormally high rating fees (and any correlation with favorable credit ratings) are observable, this transparency should work against any ratings bias favoring lucrative issuers that might otherwise arise.2

We offer clean tests of CRA bias favoring issuers with relatively expensive ratings by controlling for the portion of credit rating fees that is plausibly driven by the amount of work required to create them. We predict that CRAs produce favorable ratings for valuable issuers, controlling for issuer frequency, opacity, and all manner of issue complexity, by comparing fees and ratings across CRAs for the same bond at the same time. Our baseline analysis compares Moody’s fees and ratings to S&P’s fees and ratings for the same new bond issue by the same issuer.

We study a complete sample of issues from public issuers in the state of Texas (where fees are observable for each CRA and issue) from 1998 to 2020. Within-issue analysis (comparing credit ratings and rating fees across different CRAs for the same issue at the same time) allows us to identify the relation between rating fees and rating levels, controlling for issuer fundamentals, issuer characteristics, and the macroeconomic environment. For example, consider Farmers Branch and Frisco, adjacent cities in our sample (21 miles apart) each issuing general obligation (GO) bonds 2 months apart in 2013. Both cities hired Moody’s and S&P to rate their new issues. Farmers Branch paid S&P 168%more than it paid Moody’s and received a one-notch more favorable rating from S&P (AA+⁠) than it received from Moody’s (Aa2). Frisco paid Moody’s 237%more than it paid S&P and received a one-notch more favorable rating from Moody’s (Aa1) than from S&P (AA). Because each issue-rating observation compares ratings of the same issue at the same time, the observed difference in ratings cannot be explained by issuer fundamentals, bond characteristics, and macroeconomic conditions.3This positive correlation between credit ratings and fees, controlling for observables, suggests that issuers pay more for higher ratings and/or that CRAs understate the credit risk of issuers paying higher fees. In this case, S&P upgraded its harsher rating associated with its relatively lower fee in the following year (after the taxpayer cost of finance was set).

Our main analysis controls for expected CRA compensation for work by analyzing the relation betweenresidualfees paid to CRAs and the credit ratings they produce. We estimate residual fees as the difference between the actual fee paid and the expected fee based on fee schedule determinants provided to municipal issuers by Moody’s and S&P. We then test whether residual fees affect rating levels. In a regression analysis of the full sample, we find a significant positive relation between credit ratings and residual rating fees in a within-issue framework, holding all else constant. From this result, we conclude that conflicts of interest previously documented in corporate bond and structured finance markets also influence credit ratings quality in municipal bond (muni) markets.

However, we also find that this positive relationship between residual rating fees and credit rating levels is driven by uninsured issues. More specifically, the significant positive relationship between credit ratings and residual rating fees is driven by the subset of newly uninsured issuers who previously purchased AAA-rated coverage from bond insurers, such as MBIA or AMBAC, before these insurers collapsed during the financial crisis of 2008. These results are consistent with substitution between insurers and CRAs as certification agents in the muni market and suggest that issuers who previously purchased AAA ratings from insurance companies are incentivized to pay for favorable credit ratings when AAA insurance is no longer available.

We further test for ratings distortion stemming from the issuer-pays compensation structure by comparing credit ratings and fees for the initial rating provided by the first CRA to rate an issue and the credit ratings and rating fees for subsequently provided ratings. Rating catering predicts that CRAs notch their ratings favorably relative to their competitor’s ratings such that subsequent ratings should generally be more favorable than the initially provided rating. Because we focus on cases with multiple ratings, we are likely not capturing rating shopping where issuers select only the most favorable rating. Our results indicate (1) subsequently provided ratings (those provided second) are significantly higher on average than initially provided ratings (those provided first) across CRAs and (2) comparing each CRA to itself, ratings that are provided second (after the competitor’s rating) are significantly higher than ratings that are provided first (prior to the competitor’s rating). These results further suggest conflicts of interest in the muni rating process.

We test the robustness of our results to controls for lead credit analyst characteristics, home bias, and proximity to the issuer (a proxy for informational advantages). Statistical significance and economic magnitude of baseline results are unchanged by these additional controls. We further test robustness to analyst-level workload in case busy analysts produce less careful or more conservative ratings. We use several controls for workload including the number of sectors, number of issuers, and number of individual bonds rated by the lead analyst. In each case, we find that analysts award more conservative ratings (less favorable to the issuer) when they have less time available to certify the issuer. More importantly for our research question, our baseline evidence remains robust to these additional controls: municipal bond issuers paying higher residual fees receive more favorable ratings.

As a final test of ratings distortion associated with conflicts of interest in the muni ratings process, we analyze ratings transitions to test for differences in ex post outcomes of expensive versus inexpensive ratings. We find that expensive ratings (relative to expected fees) are more likely downgraded and inexpensive ratings are more likely upgraded suggesting the initial ratings were relatively harsh for issuers paying relatively lower fees consistent with ratings theory fromFulghieri, Strobl, and Xia (2013).

Analysis of offer yields suggests real effects of these ratings distortions to the relatively unsophisticated issuers and investors in these markets. Prior literature has established that muni investors are inattentive to public information (e.g.,Cornaggia, Hund, and Nguyen 2022), so it is perhaps not surprising that we find these investors do not price expensive ratings differently from inexpensive ratings. Because the market prices ratings at face value, municipalities paying the lowest residual fees face typical yields associated with their lower initial credit ratings (that are upgraded, on average,afteroffer yields are determined). These taxpayers therefore pay an inflated cost of capital to finance government services.Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Israelsen (2018)estimate that a one-notch difference in municipal bond ratings corresponds to 15- to 18-basis point (bps) difference in yield. For the median bond in our sample, a yield increase of 15 bps generates $212,677 in excess financing.4In contrast, because the market prices ratings at face value (regardless of likely downgrades of expensive ratings), muni investors overpay for bonds issued by municipalities who pay the highest residual fees since these bonds earn typical yields associated with the higher ratings.

Our results complement prior literature by showing how conflicts of interest in credit rating agencies manifest across asset classes and institutional settings. Prior work fromBaghai and Becker (2018)examines the relation between nonrating revenue (e.g., from consulting services) and credit ratings for corporations in India (which may face different institutional constraints than in U.S. issuers). We examine credit rating fees paid by municipal issuers to individual CRAs. These fees are not observable for corporate bonds or structured finance products. Our results indicate that conflicts of interest in the issuer-pays CRA compensation structure affects the quality of municipal bond ratings, despite the fact that our sample of municipal bond issuers face higher disclosure requirement than issuers of corporate bonds and structured finance products and the fact that public finance is less lucrative to CRAs than rating these other markets.
1.1 Credit rating agency compensation

Economic theory predicts multiple ways for the traditional issuer-pays CRA compensation model to distort ratings.Skreta and Veldkamp (2009)model credit rating shopping on the part of issuers who can select the most favorable among noisy ratings produced by unbiased rating agencies. An additional layer of conflict arises when CRAs can attract more business by producing biased ratings that understate risk to benefit marginal issuers; seeMathis, McAndrews, and Rochet (2009)andBolton, Freixas, and Shapiro (2012).5

The empirical literature indicates that CRA conflicts of interest materially distort ratings quality in corporate bonds (e.g.,Becker and Milbourn 2011;Jiang et al. 2012;Cornaggia and Cornaggia 2013;Xia 2014;Bruno, Cornaggia, and Cornaggia 2016;Baghai and Becker 2018;Kedia et al. 2017;Badoer, Demiroglu, and James 2019;Herpfer and Maturana 2020), insurance (e.g.,Doherty, Kartasheva, and Phillips 2012), and structured finance markets (e.g.,Griffin and Tang 2012; He et al. 2012;Kedia et al. 2014;Nickerson and Griffin 2017;Baghai and Becker 2020;Griffin and Nickerson 2020).Griffin et al. (2013)conclude that ratings inflation in CDOs reflects catering on the part of CRAs (understating risk to attract future business).6Results fromDimitrov, Palia, and Tang (2015)further suggest that regulatory attempts to improve rating standards (i.e., Dodd-Frank) may actually have an adverse effect on ratings quality.

Municipal bond issuers differ from issuers of corporate bonds or structured finance products in significant ways. First, the municipal asset class, as a whole, generates significantly less income for CRAs than other asset classes (Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Hund 2017). Second, compared to the large frequent issuers of structured finance products, individual muni issuers may be too small to impose pressure on CRAs.7In this case, CRAs may still face pressure from the underwriters who are repeat customers. To test for a relation between credit ratings and rating agency compensation—holding credit risk constant—we compare ratings and fees across CRAs within the same issue. This approach also holds the underwriter constant.

In the muni market,Beatty et al. (2019)find evidence that Moody’s increased its rating fees and market share after recalibrating its municipal bond rating scale in 2010. Because these authors select only issuers that hire both Moody’s and S&P before and after the recalibration, they conclude that their evidence is more consistent with CRA catering than issuer shopping. Our paper complements their earlier work with analysis of abnormal CRA fees as determinants of credit rating levels, analysis of sequential ratings and CRA fees for new issues, and tests of ratings as substitutes for lost insurance. This analysis of ratings quality in muni markets is important because this asset class is marked by poor-quality issuer disclosure (e.g.,Cuny 2016) and dominated by retail investors who heavily rely on credit ratings for information (e.g.,Adelino, Cunha, and Ferreira 2017;Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Israelsen 2018).8

1.2 Bond insurance as alternative certification

The muni market is uniquely interesting as a laboratory because of the potential substitution of certification agents in this asset class. Prior to June 2008, municipal issuers of lower credit quality could purchase AAA certification from insurers. AAA insurance provided a substitute for a AAA credit rating because muni investors priced these “purchased AAA” bonds equivalently to “true AAA” uninsured bonds as long as the insurer maintained a AAA credit rating (seeCornaggia, Hund, and Nguyen 2022). However, in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, the AAA-rated monoline insurers (MBIA and AMBAC) were downgraded and ultimately became insolvent.9With AAA insurance no longer available, many previously insured issuers began to issue bonds without insurance. We predict that issuers who previously purchased AAA certification from MBIA and AMBAC now have greater incentive to purchase AAA certification (or the most favorable ratings they can obtain) from the CRAs for their newly issued uninsured bonds even if ratings are imperfect substitutes (Bergstresser, Cohen, and Shenai 2015).

When bond insurers had AAA ratings, insured munis were priced as if they had AAA ratings; the market priced the insurer’s rating. This changed after insurers lost their AAA rating, as the market began to price the bonds based on their underlying credit quality. Because the market prices insured bonds based on the rating of the (AAA) insurance, we do not expect issuers paying for AAA insurance to also pay for favorable credit ratings. We provide evidence to support this assertion in the appendix.10Only when the AAA insurance is no longer available—and the market begins to price the underlying rating—do we expect these issuers to start obtaining (paying for) the best possible underlying credit rating. Conflicts of interest in muni ratings should be most likely to manifest at that point.
article.2 Data and Sample:
We obtain an unselected sample of municipal bond issues from the state of Texas over the 1998–2020 time period with issue information including issue amount, underlying credit ratings from each CRA hired and fees paid to each agency.11The disclosure of fees is a unique feature of the data. To our knowledge, no other asset class or setting provides information on fees paid for specific credit ratings. Therefore, these data provide an opportunity to directly test a relationship between ratings and fees. The data also include insurance premiums, an indication for whether the issue is a general obligation (GO) or revenue bond, and whether the sale was competitive or negotiated.12We translate 22-point alphanumeric ratings into numeric scales increasing in credit quality, such that AAA and Aaa ratings take the value of 22, AA+and Aa1 take the value of 21, and so forth.13For simplicity, we employ the rating nomenclature adopted by S&P and Fitch (e.g., we refer to AA+and Aa1-rated bonds as the “AA+” rating category) unless we refer specifically to Moody’s ratings.

Although they are not published, Moody’s and S&P have fee schedules they provide to issuers. From various public sources, including minutes from municipal government meetings, we obtain copies of the fee schedules from Moody’s for the years 2007 and 2010–2016 and for S&P in 2013 and 2015.Figure A.1in the appendix provides an example of a fee schedule. We located this particular fee schedule from the minutes of a town meeting held in Hailey, Idaho, in July 2010. These schedules indicate that municipal bond rating fees are generally a function of the complexity of the issue and the anticipated effort required to produce a credit rating. The observable variables listed in CRA fee schedules serve as explanatory variables in our model of bond rating fees. We then estimateResidual feeas the actual fee paid minus the fee predicted by the model.

To analyze the potential impact of residual rating fees on the cost of municipal financing, we obtain new issue characteristics from the Ipreo i-Deal database and from Mergent Municipal data. Specifically, we collect the following data for new issues in Texas over the 1998–2020 sample period: issue size, offer yield, maturity date, coupon rate, general obligation (GO) or revenue bond type, call features, and whether the new issue is negotiated or competitive, insured, and/or classified as a Build America Bond (BAB). We also obtain the number of other bonds outstanding for each issuer. We estimate credit spreads with duration-matched Treasury yields data using Treasury STRIPS data from Bloomberg.

Finally, we collect lead analyst names from analyst reports. For the subsample period 1998–2014, we collect information from LinkedIn.com and Intelius.com for each analyst name on analyst age, gender, job history and location, and educational history, including undergraduate and graduate degrees, degree granting institutions, and the number of years enrolled in each institution. If we cannot collect analyst age, we estimate it by subtracting 18 years from the first year the analyst attended college, or, if unavailable, 22 years from college graduation date.

Figure 1examines the correlation of these fees paid to different CRAs for rating the same bond issue. In panel A of this figure, we observe positive correlation between the fees paid to Moody’s and S&P across issues where both CRAs are hired. Similar results obtain for comparison of Moody’s and Fitch (panel B) and for S&P and Fitch (panel C). This correlation between CRA fees suggests that the required amount of work required to produce a credit rating varies more across issues than across CRAs within an issue. However, the scatterplots indicate many observations in which one CRA is paid significantly more than another for rating the same bond with a roughly even division between CRAs for obtaining the higher fee.

CRA fee schedules (e.g., Figure A.1 ) indicate that scheduled (expected) fees vary by year of issuance, the type of debt, the issue size, and whether or not the CRA has a prior rating relationship with the issue (akin to a frequent issuer discount). Because we have a limited number of fee schedules (from Moody’s for the years 2007 and 2010–2016 and for S&P in 2013 and 2015) we estimate expected fees using the fee determinants from these schedules across our entire 1998–2020 sample using the following model: Fee = α + β 1 Paid previous + β 2 CRA, Year, Debt type, Issue amount + ε , (1) where Fee is the dollar fee paid by the issuer to the credit rating agency for a credit rating on the issue. Paid previous is an indicator variable taking a value of one if the issuer paid for a credit rating from the rating agency on a different issue in the previous year, and fixed effects capture whether the issue rating is from Moody’s, Standard & Poor’s, or Fitch ( CRA ); the year of issuance ( Year ); whether the issue type is revenue, general obligation, or combination ( Debt type ); and par value based on $10 million bins ( Issue amount ). Residual fee as the actual fee paid minus the fee predicted by the model.

InTable 1, panel A, we see the distribution of residual fees centers around a zero mean (negative $169 median) with a standard deviation of $9,610. Sixty-seven percent of new issues are by issuers with existing relationships based on hiring the same CRA(s) to rate prior bond issues. Because we employ issue-rating observations, each new issue has potentially one, two, or three issue-rating observations. A plurality (49%⁠) of these observations are S&P ratings, 39%are Moody’s ratings, with Fitch accounting for 12%of this market share. The majority (64%⁠) of issue-rating-level observations in our 1998–2020 sample are insured. However, 56%of these issuers switch from issuing insured bonds (wrapped by AAA-rated insurance from companies like MBIA and AMBAC) to issuing uninsured bonds following the downgrades of these monoline insurers in 2008. We characterize these issuers as havingLost insurancesince AAA coverage is no longer available after June 2008.

Panels B and C ofTable 1compare the issue-rating observations with and without insurance, respectively. As expected, the average and median credit quality is higher among the uninsured. Consistent with the hypothesized substitution effect of insurers and CRAs, we observe that insured observations are associated with 30.3%lower average CRA fees ($13,667) compared to uninsured observations ($19,601).14CRA market share varies somewhat across panels B and C; Moody’s has a relatively lower share (and Fitch has a relatively higher share) of the uninsured market compared to the insured muni market.

Panels D and E divide the observations in panel C based on whether or not the issuer lost insurance. Specifically, because AAA insurance is available only before June 2008, we characterize issuers who insured their bonds prior to 2008 and then did not insure their new issues after 2008 as having “lost insurance.”Figure 2documents this lost insurance experience. In panel A, we see 96%of sample issue ratings in 2007 are for bonds wrapped with third party insurance (just prior to the collapse of MBIA and AMBAC in 2008 shown in panels B and C). By 2009, this percentage was closer to 45%⁠. By the end of our sample period, this percentage levels out around 67%⁠, with coverage primarily from Assured Guaranty or Build America Mutual (BAM), neither of whom offer AAA coverage.

As seen in panels B and C ofFigure 2, the largest public monolines became insolvent and irrelevant after 2008. Still, “lost insurance” is a choice variable; issuers who previously insured their bonds with MBIA or AMBAC prior to the crisis could continue to insure their bonds after the crisis with a lower-rated insurer, such as Assured Guaranty or BAM. In this case, these issuers and their new issues would remain in the insured subsample (panel B ofTable 1). Comparing sample sizes in panels C, D, and E of Table 1, we observe that 76.5%of uninsured issues are associated with issuers who lost their coverage, by our definition. The remaining set in panel D represent uninsured issues associated with issuers who had not previously insured their bonds, given their higher underlying credit ratings.

Figure 3displays scatterplots of raw fees paid to credit rating agencies for rating new bonds issued in Texas during our 1998–2020 sample period, categorized by underlying ratings. We plot Moody’s fees in panel A, Standard & Poor’s fees in panel B, and Fitch’s fees in panel C. Adjacent to the line of best fit in each panel are correlation coefficients and associatedp-values in parentheses to indicate statistical significance. This figure provides first evidence of significant positive correlation between the fees paid to each CRA and the ratings it produces. Expecting that raw fees are largely determined by the work required to rate new issues, we estimate in the next section the relationship between abnormal fees and credit ratings, controlling for credit risk factors in a within-issue analysis.

We test the relationship between credit ratings and abnormal ratings fees more formally with a multivariate regression and report the results in panel B of Table 2 . Residual fee is the key variable of interest in the ordinary least squares (OLS) regression of credit rating levels specified in Equation ( 2 ). Rating is a numerical translation of underlying ratings from Moody’s, Standard & Poor’s, or Fitch. It increases in credit quality, such that AAA/Aaa = 22 ⁠ , AA + /Aa1 = 21 ⁠ , and so forth. Each bond issue has one observation per rater with up to three observations per issue. Bonds rated by only one CRA (42 % of the sample) do not contribute any variation to this test. Rating = α + β Residual fee + Controls + Issue FE + ε (2)

Controls include CRA fixed effects, to control for systematic differences in CRA rating scales. We also include issue fixed effects. As such, identification comes from differences in ratings on the same bonds at the same time, not from issuer fundamentals, issue characteristics, economic fluctuations, or combinations thereof. We also include interaction terms to further control for the recalibration of municipal bond rating scales performed by Fitch in April 2010 and by Moody’s in May 2010. We cluster standard errors and the issue level.

In panel B ofTable 2we observe a significant (at the 1%level) positive relationship between credit ratings and residual rating fees across all issue-rating observations in column 1. However, comparing columns 2 and 3, we find that this effect obtains only in the set of uninsured bonds. Dividing these uninsured observations into those whose issuer never had insurance (column 4) and those whose issuer previously had AAA certification from an insurance company but then lost it (column 5) reveals that the positive relationship between ratings and residual fees is exclusive to the latter group.

Among these uninsured issues, a one-standard-deviation increase in residual fees is associated with a 0.10-notch improvement in credit ratings. Because ratings are coarse (and the minimum rating improvement is one notch), we interpret this result as one in 10 issuers paying a one-standard-deviation increase in residual fess receive a one-notch rating improvement.

The standard deviation of residual fees in this sample of uninsured issues is $11,738 (Table 1, panel E). We estimate the expected benefit of paying this increased residual fee based on expected yield improvements associated with more favorable ratings. We assume municipal bond investors price ratings at face value (a hypothesis we test inTable 7).Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Israelsen (2018)estimate that a one-notch increase in municipal bond ratings implies a yield improvement of 15–18 bps. The bonds inTable 1, panel E, have an average (median) face value of $49.0 million ($17.1 million). Conservatively, a 15-bps-yield improvement generates $25,650 annual savings on the median bond. At any reasonable discount rate, bonds with call protected duration of 6 years or longer (75%of sample) benefit from a 1-in-10 chance of obtaining a higher rating.15The median bond has call protected duration of 10 years, so the expected benefit of a one-standard-deviation increase in residual rating fees outweighs the cost for most bonds in our sample. These back-of-the envelope calculations support our hypothesis that issuers who previously purchased AAA certification from MBIA and AMBAC now have greater incentive to purchase AAA certification (or the most favorable ratings they can obtain) from the CRAs for their newly issued uninsured bonds.

Because obvious AAA issuers (e.g., Dallas County, TX) should be less sensitive to conflicts of interest in credit rating production (benefiting neither from rating shopping nor catering) compared to opaque and infrequent issuers (e.g., Magnolia, TX), we replicate the analysis in panel C ofTable 2, except we exclude observations from municipalities that issued (true) AAA bonds in the previous year. Results in panel C are comparable to those in panel B, except that the positive relationship between residual rating fees and credit rating levels in columns 3 and 5 are statistically significant at 5%(compared to 1%significance in panel B).

Finally, we examine each CRA independently in panel D ofTable 2. These results are not directly comparable to those in panels B and C because, by parsing the sample by rater, each issue has one issue rating. This approach rules out the use of issue fixed effects, so we include issuer and year fixed effects to absorb variation in the macroeconomic environment and issuer fundamentals, similar to the role of issue fixed effects in panels B and C. Given the reduced sample sizes, we add anInsuredindicator to Equation (2) rather than further bifurcate the sample based on insurance. The positive relationship between credit rating and residual fee is significant at 10%for the full sample only for Fitch (in column 5). Because transparent frequent issuers of true AAA bonds benefit least from rating shopping or catering, we exclude bonds issued by municipalities with new issues rated AAA in the previous year from analysis in columns 2, 4, and 6. In these columns focused on relatively opaque and infrequent issuers of lower-quality bonds, we see a significant (at the 5%level) relationship between credit ratings and residual fees for Moody’s as well as Fitch.

Overall, we conclude fromTable 2that (1) there is no relationship between credit ratings and residual fees among bonds where insurance provides certification; (2) among bonds lacking insurance certification, there exists a significantly positive relationship between credit ratings and residual CRA fees, which is driven by the subset of issuers who previously purchased AAA insurance when it was available; and (3) there is a substitution effect between the certification agents in the municipal bond market, that is, when opaque issuers lose the ability to purchase credible AAA certification from insurers they have new incentive to purchase certification from the CRAs.

3.3 Additional tests of rating catering

We examine next the relation between rating differences between CRAs (variation in ratings for the same bond) and the time delay between the first disclosed rating and the subsequently disclosed rating. We start with data on municipal bonds issued in the United States from June 15, 2012, to December 31, 2020, obtained directly from the CRAs’ websites.16For admittance into this sample, we require each new bond issue to have ratings at issuance from both Moody’s and S&P, that the dates their credit ratings are disclosed, and that ratings are issued on different days. Because most of this analysis does not require CRA fee data (available only in Texas), we use data for municipal issues across the United States.

Specifically, we estimate the model in Equation ( 3 ) for 9,821 issue-level observations. Rating difference = α + β 1 Moody's first + Weeks after first rating FE + ε (3)

The dependent variableRating differenceis the number of notches between the (numerically translated) credit rating disclosed second minus the credit rating disclosed first. Among all U.S. issuers, we find no significant difference between ratings disclosed within seven days of each other. Because we compare ratings of the same bonds and control for CRA-specific procedures and methodologies with a CRA fixed effect, results inTable 3likely reflect distortion in the rating process. These results are consistent with rating catering (where CRAs notch their rating higher than the competition in order to obtain future rating business). By contrast, rating shopping (where issuers select only the most favorable credit rating) is probably not driving the result because this framework requires the issue to have multiple ratings.

FromTable 4, we see that ratings assigned by each CRA that follow their competition are significantly more issuer friendly than the ratings assigned when that CRA produces the first rating. Interaction terms indicate that, for each week that passes between ratings, the second rating is about 0.11 notches higher than the first rating. Because the CRA fixed effect controls for the average credit rating level by Moody’s and Standard & Poor’s, this test controls for the predicted rating for each CRA. Controlling for the predicted rating from Moody’s, the actual rating from Moody’s is consistently higher when Moody’s rating follows S&P’s rating. Likewise, after controlling for the predicted level of S&P’s rating, the results indicate that the actual rating from S&P is consistently higher when following Moody’s rating. We believe the results fromTable 4reflect CRA catering, where either rater notches its rating higher than the competition in order to retain future rating business.

We examine the robustness and stability of the relationship between residual fees paid to CRAs and the credit ratings they produce over time inFigure 7. Here, we estimate Equation (3) year by year and plot the coefficients for the key variableResidual fee. Specifically, we replicate the specification reported in column 3 ofTable 3, panel A, based on the sample of uninsured bonds issued in the state of Texas. For practical purposes, the sample of uninsured bonds begins with the collapse of MBIA and AMBAC in 2008. In 2007, 96%of issue-rating observations were insured (seeFigure 2, panel A). Since 2008, we observe inFigure 7a positive relation between residual credit rating fees and credit ratings every year with the exception of 2015. The magnitude jumps to its in-sample peak in 2009, the first full year of data following the collapse of MBIA and AMBAC. The observation in 2009 indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in residual fees was associated with a rating increase of about 0.30 notches. Presumably, issuers were particularly shaken and motivated to secure higher ratings by paying higher fees in the immediate wake of losing insurance. However, the magnitude of the relationship between residual fees and credit ratings is stable through time. Its magnitude hovers between a zero- and 0.20-notch effect every year.

Next, we include measures of analyst-level workload inTable 5in case busy analysts produce less careful or more conservative ratings. The sample here includes all uninsured issue-rating observations for which we can obtain credit analyst characteristics for the lead analyst at Moody’s or S&P assigned to the sample issue. Because prior evidence (e.g.,Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Xia 2016;Kempf 2019;Kempf and Tsoutsoura 2021) indicates analyst-level variation in information production, we control for several analyst characteristics including gender, education, and number of years employed by the CRA she works for. Because prior evidence (e.g.,Coval and Moskowitz 2001;Malloy 2005;Butler 2008) further indicates that geographic proximity conveys an informational advantage, we include a control variable if the analyst works at a CRA office inside the state of Texas. Finally, because prior evidence fromCornaggia, Cornaggia, and Israelsen (2020)indicates a home bias among muni bond credit rating analysts, we also include an indicator for whether the analyst is from (received her social security number in) the state of Texas.

3.4 Ratings transitions

We analyze ratings transitions to test for differences in ex post outcomes of expensive versus inexpensive ratings (relative to the rating model specified in Equation (1)). Specifically, we gauge the extent to which higher ratings associated with higher residual fees results in more downgrades than higher ratings obtained with lower residual fees. First, we sort uninsured bond issues by their ratings at issuance. Second, we sort them into quintiles based on residual fees paid to CRAs. Then, we compute the average change in ratings over the subsequent 3-year period for each rating-fee category. If relatively expensive ratings are abnormally favorable to the issuer than relatively inexpensive ratings for bonds of similar credit quality, then relatively expensive (inexpensive) ratings should be more likely downgraded (upgraded) than the same ratings obtained for less (more) abnormal CRA compensation.

We tabulate the results of this exercise, separately for Moody’s (panel A) and S&P (panel B), inTable A.1in the appendix. We exclude the ratings produced prior to Moody’s scale recalibration in 2010 to prevent this recalibration from confounding our analysis. Looking first at panel A, we find in the full sample (column headed “All”) that issuers paying Moody’s the lowest (most negative) residual fees are upgraded on average while the issuers paying Moody’s the highest residual fees are downgraded on average. The 0.21 notch difference between these residual fee quartiles is significant (at 1%⁠). Separating bonds into initial rating quality, we find this notch difference is positive across broad rating categories. However, the results are strongest in the largest rating category (Aa). Here, the least expensive ratings are upgraded 0.31 notches on average and the most expensive bonds do not experience significant rating changes.

Results for S&P in panel B are similar. Among the full sample, issuers paying S&P the lowest (most negative) residual fees are upgraded on average while the issuers paying S&P the highest residual fees are downgraded on average. The 0.40 notch difference between these residual fee quartiles is significant (at 1%⁠). Separating bonds into initial rating quality, we find that the results from the full sample are similar in sign across broad rating categories. The results are only statistically significant in the largest rating category (Aa) where the difference in ratings transition between the highest and lowest residual fee quintile is 0.64 notches.

3.5 Residual rating fees and the cost of municipal financing

As a final exercise, we examine offer yields to test whether the municipal bond market prices bonds with expensive ratings differently relative to bonds with inexpensive ratings within the same rating category. Given the effects of residual fees on initial credit ratings documented inTables 2and6—where abnormally high (low) fees result in abnormally high (low) initial credit ratings—an efficient market should charge higher yield spreads to munis with inflated ratings associated with higher residual fees for any credit rating level. To the extent that markets recognize and price the ratings distortion associated with rating agencies’ fees, such distortion should not materially affect issuers or investors. However, compared to markets for other asset classes, muni markets are opaque, illiquid, require more interdealer transactions, and are dominated by relatively unsophisticated retail investors, resulting in less operational and informational efficiency.17

The dependent variable inTable 7isSpread to after-tax Treasury, the bond’s offer yield minus the after-tax yield of the duration-matched Treasury, where we calculate duration using the bond’s first call date if the bond issue is callable and the bond’s time to maturity if the issue is not callable. We assume a tax rate of 35%when calculating after-tax yields for Treasuries. If a bond issue is a Build America Bond, which is taxable to investors, we substitute its raw yield with its after-tax yield, again assuming a 35%tax rate.

Independent variables in panel A include indicator variables for Moody’s, S&P’s and Fitch’s ratings assigned to new issues. Panel B displays results from F-tests of whether the sums of regression coefficients in panel A are significantly different from zero.p-values appear below summed values in parentheses.High residual feeis an indicator variable taking a value of one if the rating from Moody’s (Standard & Poor’s, Fitch) has the highest residual fee from among the set of ratings for the new issue. Controls for bond characteristics include par value, maturity measured in years, coupon, number of bonds outstanding for the issuer, and indicator variables for whether issues are revenue bonds or general obligations, Build America Bonds, issued through a negotiated or competitive process, and callable or noncallable. We suppress these coefficients to conserve space. We cluster standard errors at the issuer level.

We find virtually no evidence that the market perceives any difference in the quality of expensive versus inexpensive ratings in the muni market. The results in panel B show that, among bonds with Aa3 and A1 ratings from Moody’s, (AA- ratings from S&P) yields are higher if Moody’s charges the highest residual fee for its rating among the set of raters. We also find that, among bonds with AA- ratings from S&P, yields are higher if S&P charges the highest residual fee for its rating among the set of raters. However, among bonds with AA ratings from Fitch, yields are lower if Fitch charges the highest residual fee for its rating among the set of raters on the bond. Among all other ratings assigned by each rater, we see no evidence that the market prices bonds differently if a rater charges a high residual fee among the set of raters on the bond.

Combined with the evidence inTable 1, panel A andTable 6that initial ratings vary by residual fees paid to rating agencies, this lack of yield adjustment inTable 7suggests real effects to the relatively unsophisticated issuers and investors in these markets. Municipal issuers paying the lowest residual fees face typical yields associated with their lower initial credit ratings (that are upgraded, on average,afteroffer yields are determined). These taxpayers therefore pay an inflated cost of capital to finance government services. In contrast, muni investors (primarily households) overpay for bonds issued by municipalities who pay the highest residual fees to rating agencies (because they earn typical yields associated with the higher ratings).
article.4 Conclusion:
Do the conflicts of interest inherent in traditional credit rating agency compensation structure distort credit ratings of municipal bonds? This question is important because the retail investors who dominate this $4 trillion opaque asset class rely heavily, if not exclusively, on credit ratings for information. Our setting provides an unselected sample of municipal bond issuers from the state of Texas with data on actual fees paid to rating agencies along with CRA fee schedules that explain how fees should vary for municipal bond ratings. We test whether issuers who pay more (less) for ratings—relative to fees predicted by the determinants disclosed in rating agency fee schedules—receive higher (lower) ratings. We find evidence in the affirmative. This result provides evidence of conflicts of interest in municipal bond ratings. Because we examine bond issues with paid ratings from multiple CRAs, we attribute our results to rating catering rather than rating shopping.

Rather than the type of coercion alleged by Jefferson County (noted in the introduction) or a simple bribe where a rating agency notches up a rating for marginal cash, we believe our results reflect a more nuanced bias. The U.S. municipal bond asset class includes over one million outstanding securities issued by 50,000 unique municipal issuers. In a repeated game, the incentives to favor issuers who consistently pay relatively high fees plausibly outweigh the potential reputation risk associated with inflated ratings. Given the clean control for credit risk afforded by within-issue analysis, there can be no credit-risk-based explanation for the relationship we find between ratings and fees.

We propose one policy prescription: the SEC has the regulatory authority to compel either the issuers of publicly traded securities or the regulated CRAs themselves to disclose the fees paid for each new bond issue. Indeed, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 requires precisely this type of disclosure for another group of certification agents (auditors) in response to Arthur Anderson’s fraudulent certification of Enron’s financial statements. Given the similarities in the incentive structures (and the inherent conflict of interest) between CRAs and auditors, a similar disclosure rule for the CRAs seems prudent. Regulating fee disclosure is unlikely to fully mitigate conflicts of interest associated with the issuer-pays CRA compensation model. Indeed, our analysis finds evidence of these conflicts even in the unusually transparent muni market in Texas. However, if the SEC mandated that all securities issuers disclose fees paid to individual rating agencies, then investors and regulators could observe and potentially discount the ratings obtained by issuers paying abnormally high fees. Given that CRA revenues from issuers of corporate bonds and structured finance products dwarf the revenues generated by municipal issuers (e.g.,Cornaggia, Cornaggia, and Hund 2017) we predict that ratings in those markets should beat leastas sensitive to abnormal rating fees. We also predict greater market response to disclosure in those markets. Corporate bond and structured finance markets are dominated by institutional investors who should be more likely than retail investors to price the expected future ratings changes associated with abnormal fees paid to rating agencies at issuance.
